text: The surgeon carefully removes midline fat from the anterior abdominal wall, exposing the glistening white posterior sheath. The dissection progresses towards the umbilicus, ensuring the mesh will sit flat against the fascia without interference from fat tissue.

The surgeon divides the umbilical ligaments to access and remove hypogastric fat. The stoma is visible, showing signs of prolapse. Adhesions around the stoma site are carefully dissected to allow full retraction of the bowel from the stoma aperture, ensuring no redundant bowel remains inside the mesh. The surgeon performs this dissection circumferentially, taking care to avoid injury to the bowel or mesentery.
The surgeon carefully retracts the stoma into the intraperitoneal space by a few centimeters, minimizing the use of cautery to avoid thermal spread.

The surgeon reduces the intra-abdominal pressure to about eight, creating less room in the surgical field. They close a two-centimeter aperture, ensuring not to incorporate the bowel or mesentery while tying it down. The stoma is lateralized, positioning the bowel laterally and wrapping mesenteric and epiploic fat around it, so the mesh contacts the fat rather than the bowel wall.

The surgeon lateralizes the bowel on the opposite side, measuring approximately seven centimeters from the midline. A 20 by 15 coated mesh is positioned with the non-coated side facing the anterior abdominal wall. The surgeon begins securing the mesh with a central midline stitch, followed by a suture along the inferior aspect of the lateralized stoma, creating a tunnel for the stoma within the mesh.

The surgeon positions the mesh over the hernia site, ensuring it lays flat against the abdominal wall. Barbed sutures are used to secure the mesh, starting from the twelve o'clock position and sewing around the edges superiorly. The use of eighteen-inch sutures allows for fewer sutures overall, despite initial difficulty due to suture redundancy.
